eyebrow.es: Breakout de alfabetización en IA generativa · Grado 7
eyebrow.vi: Breakout hiểu biết AI tạo sinh · Lớp 7
eyebrow.ar: تحدّي الثقافة في الذكاء الاصطناعي التوليدي · الصف 7
eyebrow.ur: جنریٹِو اے آئی خواندگی بریک آؤٹ · جماعت 7
eyebrow.zh: 生成式 AI 素养破解 · 7 年级

chrome.sub: An AI works great for some people — and gets things wrong for others. The reason hides in who was in its data. Crack four locks to find out who's missing.
chrome.briefP: AI learns from examples. When some groups or voices are left out of those examples, the AI knows them less well — so its answers can be wrong or unfair for them. Read the clues, then open all four locks. Each lock reveals the reasoning behind the answer.
chrome.footerText: Gen AI Literacy Breakouts · Runs entirely in your browser — no logins, no data collected.
chrome.winP: You learned that AI mirrors its training data, that under-represented groups get less accurate results, that asking 'whose examples are missing?' reveals the gap, that more representative data makes AI fairer, and how to check an output for fairness. Sharp work.

clues.title: You only learn what you see
clues.body: An AI learns from examples in its training data. It can only get good at things it saw a lot of. Whoever and whatever shows up most in the data is what the AI knows best.

clues.title: Some voices left out
clues.body: If a group of people, a language, or a place barely appears in the data, the AI has few examples to learn from. That group is UNDER-REPRESENTED — the AI knows them less well.

clues.title: Works for some, not others
clues.body: When some people are missing from the data, the AI's answers can be less accurate or even unfair for them — while working just fine for the well-represented groups.

clues.title: Whose examples are missing?
clues.body: The smartest question you can ask about any AI is: whose examples are missing? Naming who was left out helps you spot where the AI might be weak or unfair.

clues.title: More representative data
clues.body: AI gets fairer when builders add more representative data — examples from the groups, languages, and places that were left out. The goal is data that reflects everyone it will serve.

clues.title: A school event
clues.body: The spring talent show is in the gym this year, and last year a ukulele duet took first place. Fun to know — but it will not help you open any lock.

locks.title: Why It Fails Some People
locks.q: An AI tool works well for some students but keeps getting things wrong for others. What is the most likely reason?
options: The AI chose to be unfair to those students on purpose
Those students were under-represented in the data, so the AI has fewer examples of them
AI always works exactly the same for everyone, so nothing is wrong
locks.reason: AI learns from examples. When a group is under-represented in the training data, the AI has fewer examples to learn from, so its results for them can be less accurate.

locks.title: Spot the Gaps
locks.q: Check EVERY reason an AI might work poorly for a certain group of people.

items.t: That group barely appears in the training data
Examples in their language or dialect were left out
The data mostly represents other groups instead
The AI personally dislikes that group of people
The data already includes plenty of examples of that group

locks.reason: An AI struggles with a group when that group is missing or under-represented in its data. AI has no personal feelings, and having plenty of examples helps rather than hurts.

locks.title: Name the Fix
locks.q: To make an AI fairer, builders add data that better reflects everyone it will serve. Data that includes all the groups fairly is called ______ data.
locks.reason: Representative data includes all the groups an AI will serve. Better representation means fewer people are missing, so the AI's results are more accurate and fair.

locks.title: Check It for Fairness
locks.q: An AI gives an answer that seems to work for some people but not others. Tap the steps to check it for fairness in the RIGHT order.

pads.e: 👥 Ask who the answer works for

locks.reason: First ask WHO the answer works well for. Then ask whose examples are MISSING. Then suggest a FIX — more representative data. Who, Missing, Fix.
